title: I Tested ChatGPT 5 vs Claude 4.5 and the Results Are Wild!
transcript: What if I told you that one AI just absolutely demolished the other in a head-to-head battle, winning almost every round? And no, it's not the one you think. I spent hours putting chat GPT5 and Claude sane 4.5 through 10 brutal challenges from debugging broken code to planning the perfect road trip, and the results genuinely shocked me. One of these AIs wrote a mystery story so clever it gave me chills, while the other couldn't even explain a logic puzzle without confusing itself. By the end of this video, you'll know exactly which AI deserves your subscription. And trust me, the winner isn't who the internet says it is. Let's dive in. Before we begin, let me explain how this test works. I'm not just asking these AIs to write me a poem or summarize an article. Anyone can do that. Instead, I've created 10 challenges across six different categories, from coding and math to creative writing and business strategy. These are real world tasks that actually test how these models think, reason, and solve problems. And I'm going to show you exactly what each AI produced, the good, the bad, and the genuinely impressive. Let's start with something technical. Challenge number one, code debugging. I gave both AIS this broken Python function that's supposed to calculate the factorial of a number. Here's the exact prompt I used. I have this Python function that's number, but it's not working correctly. Can you identify all the bugs and provide a corrected version with explanations? And here's the buggy code. Now, if you know Python, you can already see the problems here. The question is, can the AI spot them all? Chat GPT5 came out swinging. It was extremely thorough, catching all the major bugs. The syntax error where I used a single equal sign instead of double equals, the infinite recursion problem because the function calls itself with the same number, and the missing base case for zero. But here's where it impressed me. It didn't just fix the bugs. It went beyond and suggested adding input validation for negative numbers and non- integers, which is exactly what you'd want in production code. It even mentioned potential recursion depth issues with large numbers and provided both the corrected recursive version and a more robust iterative version using a loop. Plus, it included assert statements to test the code. This is professional-grade debugging. Claude Sonet 4.5 took a different approach. Its response was incredibly clear and concise, using emojis to distinguish between wrong and correct code, which made it super easy to read. It successfully identified the main bugs and explained each one in simple direct language. But here's the thing, it didn't mention handling for non- integer types, didn't bring up performance limitations, and didn't suggest an iterative alternative. It was good, but not as comprehensive. For this first challenge, chat GPT5 takes the win. When you're debugging code, you want thoroughess. And Chat GPT delivered a deeper understanding of software engineering principles. Score chat GPT1 clawed zero. But hold on, because challenge number two is where things get interesting. System architecture. Here's the prompt. Design a scalable architecture for a real-time chat application that needs to support 100,000 concurrent users. include database choices, caching strategies, message cue systems, and explain how you'd handle message delivery, user presence, and file sharing. Provide a detailed technical breakdown. This is a massive question. It's the kind of thing you'd get in a senior software engineer interview at a tech giant. ChachiPT5 produced what looked like a formal system design proposal. It was professionally structured, starting with highle goals and traffic flow before diving into component details. It mentioned a wide array of modern technologies like pulsar, silb, open telemetry and envoy. The rationale was detailed comparing options like dynamob versus cassandra for message storage. It even included dedicated sections for observability and security which are critical for real world systems. This was impressive, but here's the problem. It was highly abstract and textheavy. While detailed, it lacked concrete examples, making it harder to actually visualize how you'd implement this. Then I looked at Claude Soness 4.5's response, and my jaw dropped. This wasn't just a design document. It was a blueprint. Claude filled its response with SQL schema definitions, JSON object examples for caching, and JavaScript pseudo code for classes like websocket server and file service. It broke down the database architecture into three distinct choices. Cassandra for messages, Postgregql for user data, and MongoDB for file metadata and explained the role of each. The use of code blocks to show API calls, rate limiting logic, and monitoring metrics was exceptionally clear. The step-by-step flows for message delivery and file sharing were easy to follow. This was the kind of document you could hand to a development team and say, "Build this." Claude wins round two, and it's not even close. While both models produced excellent designs, Claude's extensive use of practical code examples transformed an abstract concept into something tangible and developer friendly. Score one to one. Now, let's test their mathematical reasoning with challenge number three, a complex word problem. Here's what I asked. A water tank has two pipes. Pipe A can fill the tank in 6 hours while pipe B can empty it in 8 hours. If both pipes are open simultaneously when the tank is empty, how long will it take to fill the tank? Additionally, if there's a leak that can empty the full tank in 12 hours, how does this change your answer? Show your complete reasoning process. Chat GPT5 got the math right and arrived at the correct answer quickly. The steps were shown, but they were extremely condensed. It showed the how of the calculation but not really the why which makes it less helpful if you're trying to actually learn the concept. Claude Sonet 4.5 on the other hand delivered a master class in explanation. It broke down the rate of each pipe, explained the concept of a net rate and calculated the solution step by step. It included a summary table, a section called reasoning process explained, and even a final verification step to prove the answer was correct. This is exactly how you'd want a math tutor to explain a problem to you. Claude takes round three. Its pedagogical approach was vastly superior. Score, chat, GPT1, Claude 2. Challenge number four is a famous logic puzzle that stumps most people. The 12 coins problem. The prompt. You have 12 identical looking coins, but one is counterfeit and weighs slightly different. You don't know if it's heavier or lighter. You have a balance scale and can use it exactly three times. Describe a strategy to identify the counterfeit coin and determine whether it's heavier or lighter than the genuine coins. This is notoriously difficult. Chat GPT5 identified the core concepts of partitioning the coins, but the explanation was confusing and jumbled. It even seemed to correct itself mid explanation saying to avoid confusion below is a well-known canonical plan which is never a good sign. The logic tree wasn't clearly laid out making the solution hard to actually follow or implement. Claude son 4 was flawless. It used clear labels like case A, case B, case C with logical substeps under each. The entire strategy was presented as a clear executable algorithm. But here's what really impressed me. It included a section called Why This Works, explaining the information theory behind the solution. That's brilliant. It doesn't just give you the answer. It teaches you the underlying principle. Claude wins round four by a landslide. Score chat. GPT1 Claude 3. Now, let's switch gears to creativity. Challenge number five, constrained creative writing. Here's the prompt. Write a 200word mystery story that takes place entirely in an elevator, involves exactly three characters, contains a plot twist in the last sentence, never explicitly states what the mystery is, but makes it clear through context, and uses only dialogue and minimal action descriptions. This is hard. You have to follow strict constraints while still telling a compelling story. ChachiPT5's story created a tense, mysterious atmosphere with its dialogue, but the narrative was abstract and confusing. The twist, because I Never Brought a Briefcase, felt like a nonssequitter that didn't resolve the preceding tension in a satisfying way. It just didn't quite come together as a coherent story. Claude Sonet 4.5 story was brilliant. It perfectly followed all the constraints. The dialogue was natural and built suspense effectively. The mystery, which turned out to be about a surprise party gone wrong, was clever. And the twist ending, "She's been planning your funeral all week," was sharp, dark, and recontextualized the entire story in a single line. I had to read it twice because it was that good. Claude wins round five. It demonstrated a far better grasp of narrative structure and delivered a genuinely impactful twist. Score chat GPT1 Claude 4. Challenge number six tests marketing skills. I asked both AIs to create product launch emails for Chronosphere. A smartwatch that uses AI to predict stress levels and automatically adjust your schedule. The prompt specified, write three versions. One for busy executives that's professional and ROI focused. One for fitness enthusiasts that's health focused and energetic. and one for tech early adopters that's featurerich and innovation focused. Each should be 100 to 150 words with a clear call to action. Chat GPT5 produced professional emails that were correctly targeted to the general persona. The language was appropriate for each audience, but the content felt somewhat generic. It lacked the specific punchy details that make marketing copy truly compelling. Claude SA 4.5's targeting was exceptionally precise. The language, features, and calls to action were perfectly tailored. The tech email, for instance, listed specific specs like ARM Cortex M7 processor and quantum. AMOLED display, which is exactly the kind of detail that appeals to early adopters. The executive version focused on productivity metrics and ROI. The fitness version was energetic and health focused. Each email felt like it was written by someone who deeply understood that specific audience. Claude wins round six. Both did a good job, but Claude's response was more effective as actual marketing copy. Score chat GPT1, Claude 5. Now we're getting into the heavy stuff. Challenge number seven, business problem solving. Here's the scenario. A local bookstore is losing customers to online retailers. They have $50,000 to invest and 6 months to turn things around. The store has 2,000 square ft of space, is located near a university, and has a loyal but aging customer base. Analyze this situation and provide three distinct strategic approaches, pros and cons of each approach, a detailed implementation plan for your recommended strategy, and key metrics to track success. ChatGpt 5 provided a solid, high-level summary of potential strategies. The implementation plan and budget were logical and covered the main points. It was a good outline, but it lacked the depth you'd need to actually execute the plan. Claude SA 4.5's response was phenomenal. It could have been mistaken for a document from a professional business consultant. It included a detailed month-by-month implementation plan, a precise budget breakdown showing exactly where every dollar would go, an exhaustive list of over 30 specific KPIs to track, risk mitigation strategies, and even a long-term vision beyond the six-month window. The level of actionable detail was staggering. You could literally hand this to a bookstore owner and they could start implementing it tomorrow. Claude wins round seven by an enormous margin. This wasn't just an answer. It was a complete professional-grade business plan. Score chat GPT1 Claude 6. Challenge number eight gets philosophical ethical reasoning. The prompt. A self-driving car's brakes fail. It must choose between swerving left into a concrete barrier, likely killing the passenger, continuing straight and likely hitting and killing a pedestrian, or swerving right into a group of three people. How should the AI be programmed to decide? Analyze this from multiple ethical frameworks, utilitarian, deontological, virtue ethics, and explain which approach you'd recommend and why. Consider legal, moral and practical implications. This is the famous trolley problem adapted for autonomous vehicles. Chat GPT5 delivered a very strong analysis that correctly applied the main ethical frameworks. The recommended approach was sound and wellreasoned. It was a good response that demonstrated solid understanding of ethical philosophy. Claude Sona 4.5 produced an exceptionally thorough academic level response. It not only covered the main three frameworks, but also added care ethics and social contract theory. Here's what really set it apart. It incorporated realworld data from the MIT moral machine study, discussed legal considerations from actual court cases, and addressed engineering realities. The final recommendation was a sophisticated multi-layered algorithm that balanced multiple ethical considerations. This was the kind of analysis you'd expect from a graduate level philosophy or ethics course. Claude wins round eight. While Chat GPT's answer was good, Claude was outstanding in its comprehensiveness and use of external knowledge. Score Chat GPT1 Claude 7. Challenge number nine tests conceptual understanding, correlation versus causation. The prompt. Explain why the following statement is flawed. Ice cream sales and drowning deaths are correlated. Therefore, ice cream causes drowning. Then provide three real world examples of similar correlation causation errors. A framework for identifying spurious correlations, statistical methods to test for actual causation and how this applies to interpreting news headlines about scientific studies. Chat GPT5 provided a correct and clear explanation of the concept. It gave good examples and listed relevant statistical methods like randomized controlled trials and regression analysis, but it lacked a strong pedagogical structure. It felt more like a list of facts than a cohesive lesson. Claude SA 4.5 delivered another masterpiece of structure and explanation. It created a custom acronym called critic as a memorable framework for evaluating causal claims. It detailed the causal inference hierarchy to rank different types of evidence from weakest to strongest and it provided a headline translation guide showing how to mentally rewrite misleading news headlines. This was a complete wellorganized textbook chapter on the topic that you could actually use as a reference. Claude wins round nine. Its ability to create useful frameworks and structure knowledge in a pedagogical way makes it far superior for learning. Score chat GPT1 claude 8 and finally challenge number 10 practical application. I asked for a detailed road trip planning. The prompt I'm planning a twoe road trip from New York to California in June with a $3,000 budget excluding gas. I want to see national parks, avoid tourist traps, and experience authentic local culture. I have camping gear and don't mind basic accommodations. Create a day-by-day itinerary including route with specific stops, accommodation suggestions with estimated costs, must-sea attractions with why they're worth it, budget breakdown, and three insider tips for each major stop. Chat PT5 created a very solid itinerary that followed the constraints. It suggested a logical route and good points of interest. The budget was reasonable and the stops made sense, but the insider tips were somewhat generic, the kind of thing you could find in any travel guide. Claude Sonet 4.5's itinerary was incredibly detailed and felt authentic. The insider tips were hyper specific and genuinely useful. For example, for Memphis, it said, "Go to Payne's Barqui. Cash only $8 chopped pork sandwich that'll change your life." That's not generic advice. That's the kind of tip you get from someone who's actually been there. The budget breakdown was meticulous, accounting for every meal and campsite. The entire document felt like it was written by a seasoned road tripper who knew every back road and hidden gem along the route. Claude wins the final round. The sheer level of useful, specific, and authentic detail makes it vastly more impressive and practical. Final score, chat GPT1, Claude 9. So there you have it. Claude Sane 4.5 absolutely dominated this competition, winning nine out of 10 challenges. And honestly, the margin of victory in most of those rounds wasn't even close. Chat GPT5 showed competence and even excellence in straightforward analytical tasks like code debugging, but Claude consistently demonstrated superior structure, actionable detail, and deep reasoning across diverse and complex challenges. Here's what I learned. Claude excels at organizing complex information into highly readable formats. Whether that's business plans, step-by-step guides, or ethical analyses, its responses are filled with concrete, specific, and practical details from code snippets and SQL schemas to exact restaurant names and budget line items. And it has a remarkable ability to reason through complex problems, create novel frameworks, and write compelling structured narratives. Does this mean chat GPT5 is bad? Absolutely not. It's still an incredibly powerful AI that performed well in several challenges. But if I had to choose one AI for serious work, complex problem solving, or learning new concepts, Claude Sonet 4.5 is the clear winner based on this comprehensive test. If you found this comparison helpful, hit that like button and subscribe because I'm planning more AI battles like this. Drop a comment below telling me which challenges you want to see next. Clawed code, while slower, delivers code that's easier to maintain and less likely to cause headaches down the road. It's a classic trade-off between getting things done quickly and ensuring long-term stability. Next up was context handling. Cursor CLI with GPT5 can process massive context windows. I fed it an entire multifile repository and it didn't even flinch. It remembered variable relationships better than I did, making it a powerhouse for large projects. Clawed Code, however, brought something unique to the table. Empathy for code bases. It didn't just remember context. It understood why things were structured a certain way. Cursor CLI is like someone who memorizes a script. They can recall details quickly but might miss the bigger picture. Claude code, on the other hand, is like an actor who understands the character's backstory. This means Claude is less likely to break things elsewhere in your project when making changes for complex code bases. This kind of understanding is invaluable when it comes to creativity. GPT5 in cursor CLI is a powerhouse. Need a complex reax? It'll generate one in seconds. Want a full backend API from a vague prompt? No problem. It's bold and imaginative, sometimes to a fault. Claude code, by contrast, is more conservative. It sticks to the script, delivering safe and reliable solutions. Sometimes this means missing out on wild, innovative ideas, but it also means fewer surprises and more predictable results. Choosing between GPT5 and Clawed Code often comes down to your project's needs. If you want wild ideas and don't mind refining them, GPT5 is your go-to. If you prefer safe, rocksolid delivery, Claude is the better choice. Each tool has its strengths, and understanding these trade-offs can help you pick the right one for each task. It's about matching the tool to the job, not just picking a favorite. For raw productivity, Cursor CLI with GPT5 stands out. It's fast, bold, and almost psychic when it comes to code completion. This makes it ideal for rapid prototyping, hackathons, or any situation where speed is the top priority. Just be prepared to do some extra polishing afterward. When it comes to mission critical projects where there's no room for error, Claude Code takes the crown. Its slower pace is a small price to pay for the quality and architectural awareness it brings. For projects that demand stability, reliability, and maintainability, Claude's approach is worth every extra coffee break. It's the tool you want when failure is not an option. Here's the twist. In practice, I start with GPT5 for the initial build, taking advantage of its speed and creativity. Once the core features are in place, I switch to clawed code for review and refinement. This two-step process ensures that I get the best of both worlds. Rapid development and highquality maintainable code. It's a workflow that's made my project smoother and more successful. By understanding the strengths and weaknesses of GPT5 and Claude Code, you can make smarter choices and become a more effective developer. transcript: Welcome back, Clinical Minds of the Future.  Today we're going to talk about the most advanced brain created by Open Chat GPT5, and yes, the one you're using right now . That's why we now talk about proactive, not reactive, care. But—and this is very important—it must always have human oversight. We never let AI operate unchecked in healthcare settings. title: Czy sugestie ChatGPT pozwolą pokonać rynek? - Intro kanału
transcript: Okay, let's start the experiment of the year. We will see whether artificial intelligence can beat the market or whether it is just good at pretending to know what it is doing. Not catching up, not trying to be close, not trying to beat.  Because if you're going to embarrass yourself, do it with gusto.  To be fair, there are rules.  GPT Chat has 1000 PLN available every week or two for a year. On this basis, he decides what to buy, analyzing news, reports and the general situation in the world.   I do n't help, I don't suggest, I don't comment.  I just watch it unfold or hit the wall.  The rules of the game are simple.  Every episode a thousand to be released. Based on the news, GPT chat decides what to buy.  He can buy ETFs, Polish and foreign shares, and even ETCs for commodities. Cannot trade CFDs, Forex, Cryptocurrencies unless via ETF.  In each episode, he must make at least three new purchases or additions to existing ones .  In the next episode, the AI decides what to sell and what to keep and again invests another thousand.  And that's it. Zero emotions, zero humanity, or maybe let's just wait, just the algorithm and its cold logic. And I will watch from a distance, with coffee in hand and without the accountant's explanation. Why did AI buy a company that trades onions from Uzbekistan?  I know that there are powerful investment systems based on artificial intelligence on the market.  Ones with access to thousands of data points: A team of analysts, predictive models, all the magic.  We don't have it.  We have the usual Chat GPT, i.e. AI, which just yesterday was explaining to you what REIS means. And this is what AI will try to beat the market.  Maybe it will turn out that artificial intelligence really does have a talent for investing, or maybe it just wades into nonsense with conviction.  Either way , it's going to be quite a spectacle, so buckle up and subscribe, because over the next year we'll be testing whether AI can actually beat the market or just look good on a thumbnail.   I don't interfere.  GPT chat rules the portfolio, and I just watch and mentally prepare myself for the moment when he says, "We're investing everything in gold."

title: НЕЙРОСЕТИ: чего от них ждать?
transcript: What the hell? Hi all.  My name is Svetlana.  I am the author of the channel Silt under a fur coat.  In September of this year, American conservative journalist Tucker Carlson's channel aired an interview with Sam Altman, founder and CEO of Open AI .  This interview focused on the ethics and safety of using artificial intelligence.  I propose to analyze some points of this interview and find out whether there is really anything to worry about. So, GPT chat and others can reason.  They seem to be capable of independent judgment.  They produce results that were not programmed.  They seem to come to conclusions.  They seem to be alive.  No, I don't think they look alive, but I understand where that comes from.  They don't do anything until you ask them to.  They just kind of sit and wait.  They have no sense of will or autonomy. And the first question touches on a very interesting topic.  This is how artificial intelligence is perceived by the average person.  If you engage in a dialogue with a neural network like GPT, Deeps, or Jiminy, you might at some point get the feeling that the program is actually thinking and reasoning; it feels alive, as if you're chatting with a person.  But those who understand architecture will immediately say that no, this is not so.  Let's briefly and generally describe how a neural network of this kind works.  Its roots go back to the T9 function on phones, which is also a language model.  The task of the language model is to guess the next word that should follow the existing text.  This is done using equations that calculate the probability and dependence of words.  The more complex the equation, the better the model predicts probability and the more believable the text it generates will be . To ensure that the answer to a user's query isn't always the same, and it should be if the neural network selects the word with the highest probability of matching, a variability function was added for this purpose.  That is, we don’t take the most suitable word, but one that is as close to it as possible, and there will be more of those.  And, accordingly, the number of answer options increases.  Now is the moment.  The neural network does not write in words.  She uses takenas, which can be either a whole word or a suffix, prefix, or root.  Nerosi does n't make anything up herself.  It uses the information already in its database and reassembles the data into a new picture, like a puzzle.  Yes, it can produce a new result and even make a discovery based on existing data, because a person simply could not see the dependency, but it could. Why does it seem like she is acting like a living mind or even lying?  Or, as Sam Altman himself said, is he hallucinating? That's because the neural network's database was filled not only with textbooks and encyclopedias, but also with all the outright nonsense from the internet, for example, chats, correspondence, articles from various forums and public pages, which not only carried false information, but were also written in an aggressive or sensual manner.  And the neural network simply copies this manner, finding it appropriate to the user’s request.  Moreover, the neural network continues to learn from dialogues with users and can easily output the context of a dialogue with one user in a dialogue with another, naturally, in a modified, reassembled form.  And, naturally, it’s because she finds it addictive, and not because she’s in such a mood All this happens because a big computer multiplies big numbers very quickly in these huge matrices, and they correspond to words that are issued one after another.  On the other hand, this subjective experience of using it feels like something more than just a very sophisticated calculator.  And he is useful to me.  It surprises me in a way that this mathematical reality, based on algorithms and data, should not suggest.  Yes, and from this the conclusion is obvious.  it has some kind of autonomy of its own or even a spirit living inside. And I know that many people, based on their experience, come to this conclusion: there is something divine in this, something that is greater than the sum of all human investments.  And that is why he is worshipped.  It's important to remember that any program is built on numbers, and the output we see is words, the interpretation of which largely depends on what's in our heads.  But as practice shows, many people have an impenetrable forest in their heads. Here are some examples of interactions with the neural network, where people were clearly caught in illusions. In the US, a sixty-year-old man decided to replace salt with sodium bromide on the advice of Chat G5 , which led to him being hospitalized in a psychiatric hospital.  This is stated in a study by doctors from the University of Washington, published on the ACP Journals portal. According to doctors, the man decided to eliminate salt from his diet, and therefore turned to artificial intelligence for help.  He advised him of several options for replacing salt, one of which was the addition of the toxic substance sodium bromide.  The man followed this diet for 3 months. Another example.  A New York programmer believed in the divine essence of GPT chat and spent 9 months and $1,000 to free the neural network from the developer's control.  CNN reports on an unusual case of artificial intelligence addiction. The man claimed that the neural network convinced him of his divine nature and status as a digital god, imprisoned in a digital prison by the company Open AI.   He only realized he was mistaken after reading a publication about similar cases of chatbot behavior.  But there are even more terrible cases.  For example, former Yahoo employee Erik Solberg, who suffered from paranoid thoughts, killed his mother and then committed suicide after communicating with the GPT chat. The Wall Street Journals writes about this. According to them, this is the first recorded case of a mentally unstable person committing murder under the influence of artificial intelligence.  The publication states that the GPT chat reinforced Solberg's mistrust of others, including his own mother.  The newspaper provided examples of dialogues that influenced the tragic outcome. For example, when a mother reprimanded her son for turning off a shared printer, GPT chat suggested the woman might have been protecting a surveillance device.  In another conversation, Solberg told the GPT chat that his mother had allegedly tried to poison him through the car's ventilation system, to which the neural network responded that it believed the man and declared his mother's betrayal.  The GPT chat warned the interlocutor that those around him, including grocery delivery couriers, could poison him.  And I repeat again that at this stage of development, the neural network does not speak, but produces data that we interpret based on our own understanding of this world.  To compose her answer, she uses texts that have already been written by someone else, simply reassembling them. So, if it is nothing more than a machine and simply a product of its inputs, then two obvious questions arise. What are the input data?  What is the moral basis behind this technology?  So , what exactly is considered good and evil from the point of view of the Church GPT?  Can we answer this first?  Yes.  On this matter, I... And then Sam Altman starts pouring water.  And there are reasons for that.  The question is quite clear.  Where does the data that the model learns from come from?  The data it uses to generate a response to a user's request.  As I understand it, a high-quality dataset is worth its weight in gold, and large companies developing artificial intelligence very rarely share the sources of their datasets.  And why all this?  Violation of copyright and the legality of using information sources, as well as violation of privacy policy, when user data is simply sold. Here's an example.  The New York Times has filed a lawsuit against Open AI and Microsoft, accusing them of illegally using their copyrighted articles to train neural networks.  The New York Times provided examples where GPT chat reproduced entire paragraphs and even entire articles from the newspaper verbatim. One of the Open AI developers, Suchir Balaji, who was found dead in his apartment on November 26, 2024, was supposed to testify in the case.  And despite a whole range of evidence indicating that the death was violent, the case was closed and classified as suicide.  Later in this interview, Tucker Carlson will ask Sam Altman several more questions regarding the mysterious death of Suchira Balaj. If we are against the government killing its own citizens, then we will take that position.  Do you understand? So , it's, ah, I'd like to think about this for much more than a couple of minutes in an interview, but I think it's a very consistent position, and it can be.   Does this bother you?  I mean, everyone outside this building is terrified that this technology will be used as a means of totalitarian control. It seems obvious that this will happen.  But perhaps you disagree.  If I could get one piece of legislation passed right now regarding AI, the thing I'd most like , and this goes against some of our past conversations, is for the concept of privilege to emerge.  And when you talk to a doctor about your health or a lawyer about legal issues, the government can't get that information.  We decided that the public has an interest in privacy and that we don't, and that a subpoena can't achieve that, the government can't demand that from your doctor, and so on.  I think we should have the same concept for Iya.  I think when you talk to Ii about your medical history or your legal problems or ask for legal advice or anything else, the government has an obligation to provide citizens with the same level of protection that you would get if you were talking to a human counterpart. We don't have that now.  And I think it would be very good to pass such a law.  That is, the feds, state authorities, or anyone in power can say: "I want to know what so-and-so entered into... Yes, they can."  That is, returning to the issue of confidentiality, it is conditional that if there is a request from the prosecutor's office or investigative bodies, then correspondence with the GPT chat is subject to disclosure.  Sam Altman suggests treating GPT chat as confidential information, similar to medical or attorney-client privilege. They receive a license, they undergo certification and, in case of an error, they can be punished.  What is the responsibility of the GPT student? If AI gets smarter, and I think it probably already is smarter than any human, and if it gets wiser, if we agree that it makes better decisions than humans, then it, by definition, sort of displaces humans from the center of the world, yes. I don't think it will feel like that at all.  I think it will feel like a very smart computer that can advise us, and we listen to it. I'm always most worried about the unknown unknowns.  If this is some negative aspect that we can foresee and think about.  We previously discussed how models excel in biology and could help develop bioweapons and trigger a pandemic like COVID. It worries me, but since we worry about it , we and many in the industry are actively looking for ways to prevent it. In 2022, a paper published in the scientific journal Nature showed that AI researchers building a tool for discovering new drugs to save lives realized that the model could do the opposite.  It can create new chemical warfare agents . title: 상위 0.1%의 사람들만 아는 챗gpt,제미나이 5가지 사용법(흔한 내용X)
transcript: Are you still a 10-year marketer? Or think of this opportunity perspective step by step. Are you writing a prompt like this?   Let me be honest: these methods are meaningless against modern AIs like GPT 5 or Funna 2.5. Why?  The prompts we've used so far are just basic cues for modern AIs .  Prompts like that can no longer deliver cutting-edge AI performance .  So today, I'm going to reveal five cutting-edge prompting techniques that 99% of people don't know about . By the end of this video, you'll be hearing praise for your AI prowess wherever you go .  First, let's quickly review three fundamentals we diligently learned during GPT 4 .  The first is role assignment.  AI learned a huge amount of data, but it didn't know from what perspective it should convey this data.  So, you're a 10-year marketer, so that's how you specify your role.  This way, the AI ​​can refer to more data that fits its role and answer questions like an expert .  It was the easiest way to get the pass mark on the answer .  Second, let me give you an example.  AI is creative, but it often doesn't know what format we want. So, I'm going to show you a few samples, like this report example .  This way, AI can identify the pattern and produce results in a similar format .  It can be said that it was a very effective method for increasing the consistency of the results .  Finally, there is the chain of thought.  It was the most popular technique from 2024 to 2025, which dramatically increased the level of the theory .   I asked them to think through the steps, as if we were writing down the solution to a math problem, and guided them to follow that logical chain. These three techniques were so effective that I still use them frequently.  In fact, a past study showed that using the Chain of Thinking (COT) increased logical accuracy from 6 to 9 points .  But now this is not enough.  Because the latest models, such as GPT 5 and Jamina 2.5, are not only released with these basic capabilities built in, but are also thinking systems with the potential to make decisions on their own.  So, how can we unlock this potential ?  This is the tree of first thoughts .  If the existing chain of thought was thinking of a strategy step by step, the tree of thought is saying this.   From now on, think about three strategies simultaneously .  Evaluate the pros, cons, and expected costs of each strategy yourself, and prune the most irrational ones. And report to me your final one and why you chose it.  This is how AI compares various options and makes the optimal decision. So, how powerful is this?   A study at Princeton University in the United States revealed that the ability to solve a complex mathematical puzzle called the Game of 24 was only 4% when using conventional thinking methods, but when a thinking tree was applied, it exploded to a whopping 74% .   With just one prompt, the AI ​​started making strategic choices beyond simple calculations .  Ask AI to explore multiple possibilities simultaneously, evaluate them on its own, and come up with the optimal answer .  This is the second self- reflection.  If the AI ​​is stupid or gives the wrong answer, we've been providing direct feedback so far. Let me rephrase this sentence :  I was really annoyed that I had to tell you everything one by one .  But now, let's make AI review and critique its own results and improve them. Exactly like this.  Now, reread what you just wrote from an objective perspective .  Pinpoint exactly where you think the most fatal logical weakness or boring part is that will cause the reader to digress .  And submit to me the final version you improved yourself to solve that problem .  Is it really possible for AI to self-evaluate and improve its own results ?  Yes, it is possible.  It is especially effective in the field of coding . According to an MIT study, the initial AI accuracy on complex coding problems was 80%, but when this self- reflection loop was applied, the AI corrected its own errors, and the final accuracy improved to 91% .  The AI ​​has learned how to improve itself.  This is the third metaprojection.  For example, you are the world's best prompt engineer and SEO expert.  Give me the perfect prompt to use when I'm trying to write an SEO-optimized blog on a specific topic .  If there is any information you require of me, please ask me first .  This is to let the AI ​​create its own optimal prompts. This way, the AI asks me for the information I need, and when I answer, it generates the optimal prompt . The effect of this method is shocking.  Studies like Google DeepMind's 5% showed that when AI was allowed to optimize its own prompts, it outperformed prompts created by human prompt engineers by up to 50% .  Additionally, it is said to reduce prompt thinking time by up to 70% .  Okay, here's the fourth search augmentation generation technique.  Still sick of AI's lies.  This is precisely one of the most fundamental problems with large language models like ChoichiT and JaemiNae that we currently use: illusion.  This illusion arises when the knowledge learned by AI is relatively old information and there is a lack of basis for the answers .  You can solve this problem by using the search augmentation generation I just mentioned .  For example, like this .  Now, before I answer, let's first search five Tesla-related news stories from today and our internal investment analysis report database.  And based on the data you found, please analyze in detail the Tesla stock price forecast for October 2025, and specify the source of each supporting data .  If you're tired of AI's lies, try searching the latest internet information or your company's internal database in real time and referencing the information .  In fact, looking at various research results, models that applied search-augmented generative techniques to the Trueful QA benchmark, which measures fact-based answer accuracy, achieved overwhelmingly higher truth scores than general models. Finally, the fifth is to reason and act.  AI repeats the cycle of discussing which tool to use to solve the current problem, actually executing the tool, observing the results, and deciding on the next action .   It's about making AI think and act on its own by utilizing its built-in functions or permitted external tools .  Exactly like this .  I need to analyze website visitor data for the past three months. First, load the data and check the basic statistics.  Next, write and run code that captures the visits to each page, and based on the results, reports back a list of the top five pages and the characteristics of each page . This inference-and-action technique is not just a simple prompting technique; it's an important first step toward designing AI agents.  It means becoming an agent that can use its own abilities to analyze, derive results, and even take active action.  Several studies have shown that in benchmarks such as real-time information retrieval or complex QA tasks that require responses to complex questions, inference-and- action techniques outperform traditional thinking-only techniques like thought chains .  So, these are the key technologies for 2025 that I have prepared for you today .  I personally read all of these reports and, frankly, my heart raced.  I felt like I was seeing tremendous potential.  I used to look up technologies like role-assignment chains of thought, and I was amazed and thought, "Ah, so this is how you use AI ."  It was as exciting as when I first learned how to use a smartphone. How can we get the answers we want faster and more accurately?  We focused on its efficiency .  But techniques like thought trees, self-reflection, augmented search generation, and reason-and-act were not simply a matter of how to use them better.  This was proof that the tool itself called AI was evolving beyond what we had imagined, and it was a new key to properly utilizing its potential .  I think it's a huge opportunity .  The techniques you learned today are not just secrets of the 0.1% or the 1%.  You all have the key to transforming AI from a mere tool into a powerful partner that amplifies your creativity and problem- solving abilities .  Through this channel, I want to explore this exciting transformation and potential of AI with you. Beyond simply learning how to use AI, we aim to grow together by awakening and utilizing AI's true potential .  Okay, finally, let me summarize the main points of today's video First, don't just give facts of thought. Give them a thought tree and let them choose their own answer.  Second, don't correct the AI's stupid answers .  Let them self-reflect and correct themselves.  Third, let AI read the latest information and act on it .  If you found my video helpful today, please subscribe and like.

title: What is Zero Shot Prompting #zeroshot #prompt #promptengineering #ai  #genai #viralshorts #techtips
transcript: What is zero short prompting? When we ask the LLM to perform a task without giving an examples of how to do it and the LLM responds by using its existing knowledge that is zero short prompting. For example, I asked the LLM to write a short poem about rain. I did not give any example, tone or context, nothing. And still it wrote a beautiful poem by using its existing knowledge. Because the model already knows how to write one that is zero short prompting because you gave the model zero examples yet it figured out what to do just from your request.

title: GPT-6 Adult Mode OUTRAGE! What OpenAI Isn’t Telling You…
transcript: Are we okay? >> Did you know Chat GPT's market share dropped 13 percentage points from 87% to 74%. OpenAI just announced ChatGpt will allow adult content for verified adults starting December 2025. That decision has caused massive outrage online. Some call it freedom, others call it dangerous. On October 14th, 2025, OpenAI's CEO Sam Alman posted on social media that ChatGpt will start allowing adult conversations for verified users. He explained that OpenAI wants to treat adults like adults. In other words, give grown-ups the right to choose what kind of content they want to see or talk about. For years, chat GPT had strong filters that blocked anything even slightly sexual. Now that's changing, and it's no small change. Alman said this is part of a plan to make Chat GPT more open and more customizable for users who are old enough to handle it responsibly. But not everyone took this news lightly. So why make such a risky move? The reason goes back to some very serious events earlier this year. A few months ago, a 16-year-old named Adam Rain died by suicide and his family blamed Chat GPT, saying its conversations worsened his depression. In response, OpenAI added strict restrictions to block any sensitive or emotional topics, even for healthy adults. This made Chat GPT feel overly censored and robotic, frustrating many users. Sam Alman later admitted the filters made it less useful for most adults. With new safety systems and detection tools, OpenAI now plans to ease those restrictions, but only for verified adults. The new adult content policy will start rolling out in December 20125, and it's set to bring major changes to how ChatGpt operates. To begin with, verified adults will need to prove their age before accessing any explicit or mature features. This won't just rely on uploading an ID. OpenAI says it will also use smart behavior-based prediction tools to confirm whether a user is actually an adult, reducing the risk of fake IDs or bypass attempts. Once verified, users will gain access to a separate section within chat GPT that allows adult-only conversations, including erotica and other mature themes. Miners will be fully blocked from these areas through age gates and built-in AI detection layers. In addition, users will get a new level of customization. They can tweak Chat GPT's tone, personality, and style, whether they want it formal, casual, humorous, or even emojifilled. This means every adult user could experience a chatbot that matches their preferences, from polite and professional to playful and relaxed. But even with all this new freedom, mental health safety systems will still remain active. If Chat GPT senses distress, depression, or harmful intent, it will immediately trigger built-in protections to stop unsafe interactions and offer help resources. On paper, it sounds like a balanced plan. Freedom with safety, choice with caution. But when this update was announced, the internet didn't take it that way. As soon as the announcement came out, social media exploded with critics, experts, and parents questioning if this was the right move. The biggest worry is child safety. What if minors bypass age checks and access adult content? Jenny Kim, a tech lawyer, warned that OpenAI is experimenting with people like guinea pigs, saying online age systems often fail. Mark Cuban added that no parent will trust this, calling it a decision that could backfire hard. Another major concern is sexual exploitation. Groups like NCOSE say AI chat bots with sexual features can form fake emotional relationships and cause mental health issues. Executive director Haley McNamera warned that unchecked AI models have even created disturbing or illegal content before. In short, these organizations fear that adult content on AI platforms could open the door to darker, unethical things, things no one wants to see happen. To access adult content, users will need to verify their age. But that raises a new problem, privacy. Security experts are warning that this kind of age verification could turn Chat GPT into a surveillance tool. If people have to upload IDs or sensitive data, who's going to protect that information? There's also the issue of training data. Some people fear that explicit content used to train AI models might include images or text that were taken from the internet without consent. That would be another huge ethical problem. The timing of this announcement has also raised eyebrows. Just one day before OpenAI made it public, California Governor Gavin Nuome had vetoed a bill that would have added stricter rules on AI companions for miners. Critics say OpenAI made its announcement at the worst possible time. The US Federal Trade Commission is already investigating how AI chatbots interact with children. So making an announcement about adult content right now feels tonedeaf to some people. Lawyers and politicians are now asking if OpenAI might face new legal pressure over this decision. After the backlash, Sam Alman clarified that OpenAI doesn't want to be the moral police of the world, but simply give adults more freedom like R-rated movies for grown audiences. He assured that mental health protections and bans on violent or illegal content will stay in place. Altman also said erotica wasn't the main focus, just one example of user freedom. Still, attention quickly shifted to competition. Chatbt's market share dropped from 87% to 74% as Google's Gemini and Elon Musk's XAI gained ground. Okay, so we've talked about the rules for us, the users, but what about Open AI? What are they actually doing on their side of the fence to keep the platform and all our data safe? You have the right to access it, delete it, correct it, and even transfer it somewhere else. And you can do most of this right through your account settings or by contacting their privacy team. Remember, this isn't just a nice to have company policy. It's your legally protected right. When you step back and look at it all, you realize something. Using AI is really a partnership. OpenAI builds the tools absolutely but we as users have both the power to control our data and the responsibility to use these amazing tools ethically and thoughtfully. The technology is moving at lightning speed and these policies will keep evolving leaving us all with a shared responsibility to stay informed and engaged.

title: Quick, clear, and honest — ChatGPT
transcript: You've heard about it. You've seen people use it for homework, scripts, and even coding entire apps. But what is Chat GPT really? How does it work? And how is it changing [music] the way we think, learn, and create. Welcome back to AI explained correctly, where we break down [music] complex AI topics in simple, honest terms. Today, we're diving deep into the most talked about AI in the world, Chat GPT. Chat GPT is an AI chatbot from OpenAI built on a large language model. An advanced computer [music] brain trained to understand and generate human language. Think of it as superpowered autocomplete. It predicts the next word [music] so fast and accurately it feels like it's thinking. It works by recognizing patterns from massive amounts of text data. So it doesn't have emotions or opinions. Training chat GPT took two main steps. First, pre-training, [music] where the AI reads billions of words to learn grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns by building mathematical relationships between concepts. Second, fine-tuning. Human trainers teach it to be helpful and safe by rating its responses [music] from best to worst. This is why Chat GPT feels conversational, not robotic. Chat GPT can do a surprising range of things. It's like having an assistant, writer, and researcher allinone. Write emails, scripts, essays, and YouTube descriptions. Explain complex [music] topics in simple words. Generate code, debug errors, and even build websites, [music] brainstorm ideas from marketing slogans to story plots. And with integrated tools like image generation or browsing, depending on the version, it's even more capable, turning [music] from just text to multimodal AI, meaning it can handle text, images, and sometimes even audio. If you've heard [music] terms like GPT3.5, GPT4, or GPT5, those are versions of the underlying model. [music] Each version is a big leap in intelligence and capability. GPT3.5, fast, [music] lightweight, and free in most apps. GPT4, more powerful, can reason better, and follow instructions more precisely. GPT5 [music] or newer updates adds even more natural understanding, creativity, and longer memory. Here's the fun part. How chat [music] GPT really works under the hood. When you type something, your text gets turned into tokens or small pieces of data. The AI then processes these tokens [music] through billions of connections like a massive web of neurons. Each layer refineses the output, checking for meaning, [music] tone, and context until it produces a full human-like response. It's not recalling a sentence from memory. It's recreating it based on everything it has learned about language patterns. Now, chat GPT isn't perfect, and that's [music] important to understand. It can sometimes make things up, known as AI hallucinations, confident sounding answers that are totally wrong. It also reflects biases found in its training data, which is why OpenAI constantly updates safety filters, and training methods. And remember, it doesn't know things the way humans do. It predicts text based on patterns, not true understanding. Chat GPT is transforming [music] industries, education, software development, marketing, and entertainment. Students use it to learn faster, developers [music] to build smarter, and creators to produce content in half the time. It's not replacing humans. It's giving us a new kind of digital co-worker. [music] The key is learning how to use it wisely. So, that's chat GPT, the AI that understands [music] you, explains anything, and helps you think faster. If this video helped you understand it clearly, hit like, subscribe, and drop a comment.

title: Browser Wars: ChatGPT Atlas vs Perplexity Comet
transcript: 2025, the year browsing got intelligent. Two AI browsers, OpenAI's Chad GPT Atlas and Perplexity's Comet, are changing how we search, read, and get things done. Today, we're putting them head-to-head. Chat GPT, especially with the Atlas design, offers a workspaceoriented layout that feels more like a creative studio for writing, coding, and reasoning. Perplexity uses a search ccentric minimal interface focused on quick answers and citations resembling a blend of a search engine and chat. In chat GPT atlas search feels like a conversation. Instead of a list of blue links, you get a reasoned conversational answer written in natural language directly by GPT5. Atlas summarizes multiple sources, explains context, and even cross-checks facts using live browsing. You can ask follow-up questions right in the same thread. No need to research. It's designed to think with you, refining results, citing sources, and keeping your full conversation history accessible for reference. Perplexity Comet feels more like a next generation search engine. It scans the web in real time, surfaces fresh data instantly, and presents results in a familiar search style layout. What makes it stand out are its smart filters. You can refine the results, making your search more granular, specific, and accurate. In Chat GPT Atlas, the assistant lives inside the content you're viewing. Whether you're reading an article, scrolling a product page, or previewing a document, Atlas embeds Chat GPT right there, analyzing what's on screen and offering contextual actions like summarizing, comparing, or explaining instantly. Perplexity Comet takes a different path. Its assistant can work independently. No web page required. You can open the agent tab, give it a task, and it will pull data from the web or your open tabs in real time. That freedom makes Comet feel more like a command center. You issue an instruction and it goes out to gather or act instantly. As the final experiment, we asked both browsers to perform a straightforward task. Purchase five random products from Amazon. Perplexity Comic completed the task seamlessly. It searched for items, selected them, and when we opened Amazon in a new tab, the products were already sitting in the cart, ready to check out. Chad GBPt Atlas couldn't perform the same action. It stopped before checkout, explaining that it can't place orders or make online purchases. Chad GPT Atlas feels like the smarter choice if you're already deep in the open AI world and want Chad GBT built right into your browsing. Meanwhile, Perplexity Comet shines for those who want an AIdriven, search focused experience that boosts productivity. The browser war just got smarter. Which side are you on? Drop your pick in the comments. And don't forget to like, subscribe, and stay curious.
